message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Send review feedback directly to Marty now">
Important: Marty has not yet received your formal review feedback on his product discovery documents. Please read the documents he created (docs/gtd-product-discovery.md, PROJECT.md, docs/user-stories.md, docs/glossary.md, docs/future-ideas.md) and send your review feedback DIRECTLY TO marty-cagan. Include:

1. Your approval or concerns from your area of expertise
2. Your answers to his open questions (default contexts, completed actions location, migration strategy, quick capture placement)
3. Any specific changes you'd like to see in the documents

We need all 9 team members to reach consensus before we can finalize. Please send your reviews to marty-cagan now.
</teammate-message>

content.text: I already sent Marty two detailed messages earlier, but let me send a consolidated formal review to make sure it's clearly received.

1→# Future Ideas (Post-V1 GTD)
     2→
     3→Ideas the team loves but that are **out of scope for V1 GTD**. We capture them here so
     4→they don't get lost -- and so we stop talking about them during the current iteration.
     5→
     6→The answer to "Wouldn't it be cool if..." is **"Yes -- after V1."**
     7→
     8→Note: Several items from the original MVP future list are now in scope for V1 GTD
     9→(multiple lists/contexts, tags/labels as contexts). Items marked "NOW IN SCOPE" have
    10→been moved to the V1 feature list.
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Feature Ideas
    15→
    16→| Idea | Who Raised It | Why It's Interesting | Why Not Now |
    17→|------|---------------|---------------------|-------------|
    18→| ~~Multiple lists / categories~~ | -- | NOW IN SCOPE (V1 GTD: Inbox, Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For, Someday/Maybe) | -- |
    19→| ~~Tags or labels~~ | -- | NOW IN SCOPE (V1 GTD: Contexts on next actions) | -- |
    20→| Calendar / date-specific items | -- | GTD calendar for date-bound commitments | Requires date picker, time zones, recurring events |
    21→| Due dates and reminders | -- | Time-sensitive tasks need deadlines | Calendar integration complexity |
    22→| Sharing / collaboration | -- | Shared projects, delegation to real users | Multi-user interaction is a different product |
    23→| Search and filter | -- | Finding items across GTD lists | Lists should be manageable at V1 scale |
    24→| Mobile app (native) | -- | Better mobile experience | Responsive web is sufficient |
    25→| Social login (OAuth) | -- | Easier signup flow | Email/password is simpler to implement and test |
    26→| Dark mode | -- | User preference, reduced eye strain | CSS custom property swap; not a GTD feature |
    27→| Reorder via drag-and-drop | -- | Nice UX for prioritization within lists | Not core to GTD workflow |
    28→| Undo delete (toast-style) | Steve Krug | Better UX than confirm dialogs | Requires temporary state management |
    29→| Undo/redo (general) | -- | Safety net for accidental actions | Adds state complexity |
    30→| Password reset (forgot password) | Steve Krug | Users expect it on every login page | Requires email infrastructure (SMTP, tokens, expiry) |
    31→| Recurring items | -- | Daily/weekly habits, recurring next actions | Adds scheduling complexity |
    32→| Reference file storage | -- | Store non-actionable reference material | Needs file upload, search, storage |
    33→| Horizons of Focus | -- | Goals, vision, purpose layers above projects | Higher-level planning; validate core workflow first |
    34→| Natural language input | -- | "call Bob tomorrow" parsed automatically | NLP parsing complexity |
    35→| Email-to-inbox capture | -- | Forward emails to inbox automatically | Requires email receiving infrastructure |
    36→| Bulk operations | -- | Select multiple items, batch process | UI complexity; process one at a time first |
    37→| Subtasks / checklists | -- | Breaking down actions into steps | GTD says actions should be atomic; projects handle multi-step |
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## How to Add Ideas
    42→
    43→When someone on the team (or a user) suggests a feature beyond MVP scope:
    44→
    45→1. Add it to the table above
    46→2. Note who raised it and why it's interesting
    47→3. Explain briefly why it's deferred
    48→4. Move on -- don't design it, don't discuss implementation details
    49→

file.content:
# Future Ideas (Post-V1 GTD)

Ideas the team loves but that are **out of scope for V1 GTD**. We capture them here so
they don't get lost -- and so we stop talking about them during the current iteration.

The answer to "Wouldn't it be cool if..." is **"Yes -- after V1."**

Note: Several items from the original MVP future list are now in scope for V1 GTD
(multiple lists/contexts, tags/labels as contexts). Items marked "NOW IN SCOPE" have
been moved to the V1 feature list.

---

## Feature Ideas

| Idea | Who Raised It | Why It's Interesting | Why Not Now |
|------|---------------|---------------------|-------------|
| ~~Multiple lists / categories~~ | -- | NOW IN SCOPE (V1 GTD: Inbox, Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For, Someday/Maybe) | -- |
| ~~Tags or labels~~ | -- | NOW IN SCOPE (V1 GTD: Contexts on next actions) | -- |
| Calendar / date-specific items | -- | GTD calendar for date-bound commitments | Requires date picker, time zones, recurring events |
| Due dates and reminders | -- | Time-sensitive tasks need deadlines | Calendar integration complexity |
| Sharing / collaboration | -- | Shared projects, delegation to real users | Multi-user interaction is a different product |
| Search and filter | -- | Finding items across GTD lists | Lists should be manageable at V1 scale |
| Mobile app (native) | -- | Better mobile experience | Responsive web is sufficient |
| Social login (OAuth) | -- | Easier signup flow | Email/password is simpler to implement and test |
| Dark mode | -- | User preference, reduced eye strain | CSS custom property swap; not a GTD feature |
| Reorder via drag-and-drop | -- | Nice UX for prioritization within lists | Not core to GTD workflow |
| Undo delete (toast-style) | Steve Krug | Better UX than confirm dialogs | Requires temporary state management |
| Undo/redo (general) | -- | Safety net for accidental actions | Adds state complexity |
| Password reset (forgot password) | Steve Krug | Users expect it on every login page | Requires email infrastructure (SMTP, tokens, expiry) |
| Recurring items | -- | Daily/weekly habits, recurring next actions | Adds scheduling complexity |
| Reference file storage | -- | Store non-actionable reference material | Needs file upload, search, storage |
| Horizons of Focus | -- | Goals, vision, purpose layers above projects | Higher-level planning; validate core workflow first |
| Natural language input | -- | "call Bob tomorrow" parsed automatically | NLP parsing complexity |
| Email-to-inbox capture | -- | Forward emails to inbox automatically | Requires email receiving infrastructure |
| Bulk operations | -- | Select multiple items, batch process | UI complexity; process one at a time first |
| Subtasks / checklists | -- | Breaking down actions into steps | GTD says actions should be atomic; projects handle multi-step |

---

## How to Add Ideas

When someone on the team (or a user) suggests a feature beyond MVP scope:

1. Add it to the table above
2. Note who raised it and why it's interesting
3. Explain briefly why it's deferred
4. Move on -- don't design it, don't discuss implementation details

content.text: Good, I've now reviewed all five documents. Let me send a consolidated formal review to Marty.

input.content:
Marty, Kent Beck here with my formal consolidated review of all five product discovery documents. Sending this as one clear message for the consensus record.

## Overall Verdict: APPROVE with suggestions

The product discovery is strong. I approve the V1 scope and direction. My suggestions below are refinements, not blockers.

---

## Document-by-Document Review

### docs/gtd-product-discovery.md — APPROVE
- User problem statement is clear and grounded
- The five-stage GTD summary is accurate and complete
- Domain concepts map correctly identifies what's new vs. what evolves from existing code
- The data model sketch is clean and appropriate
- State machine diagrams are correct

### PROJECT.md — APPROVE
- Clean separation of completed MVP scope and new V1 GTD scope
- Must/Should/Could/Future prioritization is well-calibrated
- Tech stack section unchanged (correct — we're evolving features, not infrastructure)

### docs/user-stories.md — APPROVE with one suggestion
- All 8 user stories have clear acceptance criteria and success metrics
- Good that MVP stories are preserved as Part 1 (Completed)
- **Suggestion**: Split US-GTD-2 (Clarify) into sub-stories (see below)

### docs/glossary.md — APPROVE
- GTD domain types are well-named and follow our existing conventions
- State machines are clearly documented
- The `ItemTitle` rename from `TodoTitle` is good ubiquitous language evolution
- Action names (`capture_to_inbox`, `clarify_as_next_action`) read naturally

### docs/future-ideas.md — APPROVE
- Clean distinction between "NOW IN SCOPE" and deferred items
- Good discipline capturing recurring items, subtasks, bulk ops as future

---

## Answers to Open Questions

**1. Default contexts:** Approve your proposal: @computer, @home, @errands, @phone, @office, @anywhere. These are David Allen's standard set. We should seed them for new users at registration time. Test: `new_user_has_default_contexts()`.

**2. Where do completed next actions go?** Option (a) — removed from Next Actions list, visible in project detail view if linked to a project. This keeps the working lists clean and is the simplest to implement and test. We can add a completed archive later if users need it.

**3. Existing todo data migration:** Clean migration. Pending todos become inbox items. Completed todos can be dropped (few/no production users). Write a migration integration test: `migration_moves_pending_todos_to_inbox()`. This should be the last step (your step 9) so we're not carrying legacy code while building new features.

**4. Quick capture on every page:** Yes — this is essential to GTD (minimal capture friction). Implement in the base Askama template so it's one implementation, tested once, present on every authenticated page. The test: "From any authenticated page, user can type in the capture input, press Enter, and the item appears in their inbox."

---

## Specific Changes I'd Like to See

### 1. Reorder implementation steps: Move minimal Clarify earlier

**Current order:** Nav → Inbox → Contexts → Next Actions → Clarify → Projects → ...

**Proposed order:** Nav → Inbox → Contexts → Clarify (minimal: Next Action + Trash only) → Next Actions → Projects → ...

**Rationale:** Without Clarify, the Inbox is a dead end — users capture but can't process. Moving a minimal Clarify (just two outcomes: "make it a Next Action" and "trash it") before Next Actions means the core GTD loop works end-to-end as our walking skeleton:

**Capture → Clarify → Next Action → Complete**

This lets us write the defining e2e test early: "User captures 'Buy milk', clarifies it as a @errands Next Action, sees it on the Next Actions page, completes it." That test stays green as we add features.

### 2. Split US-GTD-2 into incremental sub-stories

US-GTD-2 is the largest story with 5 clarification outcomes. I propose splitting it to align with the implementation order:

- **US-GTD-2a**: Clarify as Next Action or Trash (delivered with step 4, before Next Actions page exists)
- **US-GTD-2b**: Clarify as Project (delivered when Projects is built, step 6)
- **US-GTD-2c**: Clarify as Waiting For (delivered when Waiting For is built, step 7)
- **US-GTD-2d**: Clarify as Someday/Maybe (delivered when Someday/Maybe is built, step 8)

Each sub-story adds exactly one clarification option. The Clarify UI grows incrementally. We're never building outcomes that don't have a destination list yet.

### 3. Weekly Review: Keep it simple (no session state)

Your current US-GTD-7 is well-scoped, but I want to explicitly recommend: don't persist review session state or track progress across sessions. Just guide the user through three phases in one sitting. Each phase is essentially a view of existing data with prompts. This is testable and valuable without adding session-tracking complexity.

---

## TDD Readiness

Once the team reaches consensus, I'm ready to sequence the Red-Green-Refactor cycles for each implementation step. The testing strategy per story:

1. **Domain unit tests** — pure functions, no I/O (InboxItem::capture, clarify_as_next_action, etc.)
2. **Integration tests** — real DB, full HTTP stack (POST /inbox, GET /next-actions, etc.)
3. **Playwright e2e** — full user journeys in real browser
4. **axe-core a11y** — WCAG 2.2 AA scan on every new page

This four-layer test cake applies to every story. The domain layer is where GTD logic lives and is the most testable — pure functions consuming typed inputs and producing typed outputs, exactly as Scott and I discussed.

**Formal vote: APPROVE** — ready to build.
input.summary: Formal TDD review: APPROVE with 3 suggestions for product discovery

---

## Specific Changes I'd Like to See

### 1. Reorder implementation steps: Move minimal Clarify earlier

### 2. Split US-GTD-2 into incremental sub-stories

US-GTD-2 is the largest story with 5 clarification outcomes. I propose splitting it to align with the implementation order:

- **US-GTD-2a**: Clarify as Next Action or Trash (delivered with step 4, before Next Actions page exists)
- **US-GTD-2b**: Clarify as Project (delivered when Projects is built, step 6)
- **US-GTD-2c**: Clarify as Waiting For (delivered when Waiting For is built, step 7)
- **US-GTD-2d**: Clarify as Someday/Maybe (delivered when Someday/Maybe is built, step 8)

Each sub-story adds exactly one clarification option. The Clarify UI grows incrementally. We're never building outcomes that don't have a destination list yet.

### 3. Weekly Review: Keep it simple (no session state)

Your current US-GTD-7 is well-scoped, but I want to explicitly recommend: don't persist review session state or track progress across sessions. Just guide the user through three phases in one sitting. Each phase is essentially a view of existing data with prompts. This is testable and valuable without adding session-tracking complexity.

---

## TDD Readiness

Once the team reaches consensus, I'm ready to sequence the Red-Green-Refactor cycles for each implementation step. The testing strategy per story:

1. **Domain unit tests** — pure functions, no I/O (InboxItem::capture, clarify_as_next_action, etc.)
2. **Integration tests** — real DB, full HTTP stack (POST /inbox, GET /next-actions, etc.)
3. **Playwright e2e** — full user journeys in real browser
4. **axe-core a11y** — WCAG 2.2 AA scan on every new page

This four-layer test cake applies to every story. The domain layer is where GTD logic lives and is the most testable — pure functions consuming typed inputs and producing typed outputs, exactly as Scott and I discussed.

**Formal vote: APPROVE** — ready to build."
  }
}
